text: The surgeon begins dissecting the mid and lower thoracic esophagus. The thoracic duct is visible near the aortic arch. The surrounding periaortic tissue is noted, but not removed in this early-stage esophageal cancer case.
In the thoracic cavity, the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to control small branches connecting the descending aorta to the esophagus. The left lung is visible adjacent to the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the parabiotic tissue near the thoracic duct, ensuring the duct remains intact and undamaged during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the left vagus nerve and opens the space between the nerve and the esophagus. The left vagus nerve is cut, revealing some lymph nodes in the area, identified as left hilar lymph nodes, which are not removed as they are considered pulmonary lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies the pulmonary vein and adjacent lymph nodes, noting a potential injury to the sinister pulmonary vein. The pulmonary ligament is removed, revealing shared lymph nodes between the lung and esophagus.
The surgeon operates in a straightforward area using an energy device, with visible anatomical structures including the thoracic esophagus and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the area of diaphragmatic lymph nodes, preparing to cut the right vagus nerve after dividing the pulmonary branch. The esophagus is visible on the side.
The surgeon identifies an injury to the lung, noting the need for future suturing. They also examine a structure, possibly a Nereidspazial node, identified as a nine R lymph node.
Then, we should put it up, put it down. Okay? Okay. Can you wipe the camera for me? Okay.

The surgeon removes a diaphragmatic lymph node, identified as number fifteen, from a common area of metastasis near the diaphragm. The lymph node is turned ninety degrees for better access and visualization.
